feeConfigUnderTest.source: Kalshi published taker fee schedule, CONFIRMED against seven authenticated UFC Quick Order tickets (2026-07-16): $100 x3 at prices 0.59/0.69/0.89, plus $2/$5/$50/$500 at 0.59 which vary size at a fixed price across a 251x span
feeConfigUnderTest.note: quadratic in price: maximal near $0.50, minimal at the wings

verifiedScope.establishes: taker rate 0.07 and ceil-to-cent rounding, for single-price YES fills on KXUFCFIGHT as of 2026-07-16. Seven authenticated Quick Order tickets reproduce exactly (diff 0.00). The admissible rate interval under ceil is (0.06995195, 0.07000213] — 50ppm wide, containing 0.07 as its only two-decimal value. ceil is discriminated: floor fits 0/7, round-half-up fits 4/7 — it reproduces the four tickets whose exact cents happen to land above the .5 boundary, so ceil remains the ONLY mode fitting all seven, but the $2 and $5 tickets both fit round-half-up and therefore WEAKEN that discriminator rather than strengthening it. LINEARITY IN CONTRACTS IS MEASURED over a 251x span at a fixed price, not assumed: at 0.59, 3.28 -> 8.23 -> 82.37 -> 164.76 -> 823.81 contracts (251.16x end to end) produce exact pre-ceil fees 0.055540 -> 0.139359 -> 1.394771 -> 2.789881 -> 13.949575 (251.16x), refuting every rival exponent k*gross^x for x in 0.8-1.5. The ceil premium is observed across the whole band rather than inferred at its ends: it adds 8.03% of the fee at 3.28 contracts, 0.46% at 8.23, 0.375% at 82.37 and 0.003% at 823.81 — the small-order regime is measured, not extrapolated. The $2 and $5 tickets do NOT tighten the rate (their own intervals are 12603ppm and 5023ppm wide, since a 6-cent fee has coarse resolution); their value is size coverage only.
doesNotEstablish: MAKER fees. No maker example exists. `makerRate` has been REMOVED rather than left at an untested 0.0 — see below.
Settlement/exercise fees — a separate Kalshi line item the order ticket never displays.

Linearity in `contracts` at prices OTHER than 0.59. Size was varied at one price only, so the separable form k*C*P*(1-P) is measured on its two axes independently — linear in C at P=0.59, quadratic in P at C~111-165 — and the cross-term is inferred from separability rather than observed. A non-separable rival (an exponent on C that itself varies with P) is not excluded, though nothing suggests one.

The P=0.50 peak — the very figure that motivates this gate. Tested p(1-p) is 0.2419/0.2139/0.0979; the 0.2500 maximum is never observed. The '1.75c at 50c' number is interpolated from the fitted model, not measured.
Prices outside 0.59-0.89. Below 0.59 is covered only by the formula's own p<->1-p symmetry, which cannot validate itself.
The NO side. Symmetry makes NO@0.31 identical to YES@0.69 only if Kalshi bills side-agnostically, which no YES-only example tests.

Sizes below 3.28 contracts, where the ceil premium keeps growing as size shrinks — it is already 8.03% of the fee at the 3.28 floor. NOTE THE UNIT: the floor is in CONTRACTS, so a fixed dollar stake falls out of the envelope as price rises. A $2 entertainment order is inside at 0.59 (3.28 contracts) but OUTSIDE at 0.70/0.80/0.89 (2.78/2.46/2.22 contracts). $3 and $5 orders are inside across the whole 0.59-0.89 band.
Multi-fill ladders — the actual production path. priceOrder applies ONE ceil to the blended average price; every ticket is a single-price fill. Direction is not uniformly conservative.
Any series other than KXUFCFIGHT (other series carry different published rates), or any date other than 2026-07-16.
verifiedScope.examples: data/fee-examples.json — seven unsubmitted Kalshi UFC Quick Order tickets ($2, $5, $50, $100 x3, $500)

feeConfigUnderTest.makerNote: maker fees are unverified AND unimplemented; a maker order is refused, not guessed

examples.marketTitle: Will Dricus Du Plessis win the Du Plessis vs Usman professional MMA fight scheduled for Jul 18, 2026?
examples.source: authenticated Kalshi UFC Quick Order ticket, $100, NOT submitted
examples.note: contract price 0.69 equals the live yes_ask at collection time, independently confirming this is a taker buy at the ask rather than the post-fee effective chance

examples.marketTitle: Will Tommy McMillen win the McMillen vs Montes professional MMA fight scheduled for Jul 18, 2026?
examples.source: authenticated Kalshi UFC Quick Order ticket, $100, NOT submitted
examples.note: nearest to 0.50, where the quadratic fee is largest — this example pins the rate's upper bound most tightly

examples.marketTitle: Will Alden Coria win the Coria vs Nicoll professional MMA fight scheduled for Jul 18, 2026?
examples.source: authenticated Kalshi UFC Quick Order ticket, $100, NOT submitted
examples.note: deep favourite, near the wing where a wrong rate hides — the fee here is a quarter of the 0.59 example despite a larger stake

examples.marketTitle: Will Tommy McMillen win the McMillen vs Montes professional MMA fight scheduled for Jul 18, 2026?
examples.source: authenticated Kalshi UFC Quick Order ticket, $500, NOT submitted
examples.note: THE SIZE-CONFOUND TEST. Same market and same price (0.59) as the $100 example, 5x the size. The first three tickets were all $100, so contracts was not an independent observation — C = (100 - fee)/price made size a mechanical function of price, and rivals of form k*gross^x with x in 0.8-1.5 fitted all three. This point holds price fixed and varies size alone: 164.76 -> 823.81 contracts is a 5.0001x size ratio producing a 5.0000x fee ratio, refuting every exponent but 1.0 (x=0.8 predicts 10.11, x=1.5 predicts 31.19, actual 13.95). Linearity in contracts is measured here, not assumed.

examples.marketTitle: Will Tommy McMillen win the McMillen vs Montes professional MMA fight scheduled for Jul 18, 2026?
examples.source: authenticated Kalshi UFC Quick Order ticket, $50, NOT submitted
examples.note: THE SMALL-SIZE TEST, and the smallest quantity verified. Same market and price (0.59) as the $100 and $500 tickets, so linearity in contracts is now measured across a 10x span at a fixed price: 82.37 -> 823.81 contracts (10.0013x) produces exact pre-ceil fees 1.394771 -> 13.949575 (10.0013x), identical to 6 decimal places. This ticket also observes the ceil premium directly rather than inferring it: ceil adds 0.375% of the fee at 82.37 contracts versus 0.003% at 823.81, confirming the discretization grows at small size. Practically decisive: at a $10k bankroll the 0.5% position cap proposes ~85 contracts, which previously sat BELOW the verified floor of 111.49 and now sits inside it. Note this ticket does NOT tighten the rate — a $1.40 fee has only 140 cents of resolution, so its own admissible rate interval is 502ppm wide against the 50ppm intersection from the larger tickets. Its value is size coverage, not rate precision.

examples.marketTitle: Will Tommy McMillen win the McMillen vs Montes professional MMA fight scheduled for Jul 18, 2026?
examples.source: authenticated Kalshi UFC Quick Order ticket, $2, NOT submitted
examples.note: THE SMALL-ORDER FLOOR. Sets the verified size floor at 3.28 contracts and puts the $2 entertainment tier inside the envelope AT 0.59 ONLY — the floor is in CONTRACTS, so $2 buys just 2.22 contracts at 0.89 and falls back outside. Measures the ceil-dominated regime directly: ceil adds 8.03% of the fee here versus 0.003% at 823.81. Does NOT tighten the rate — a 6-cent fee admits rates from 0.063 to 0.076 (12603ppm) against the 50ppm intersection.

examples.marketTitle: Will Tommy McMillen win the McMillen vs Montes professional MMA fight scheduled for Jul 18, 2026?
examples.source: authenticated Kalshi UFC Quick Order ticket, $5, NOT submitted
examples.note: Fills the gap between the $2 floor and the $50 ticket. With five points at 0.59 spanning 3.28 -> 823.81 contracts (251.16x), the exact pre-ceil fee ratio is 251.16x — identical — so linearity in contracts is measured across the entire verified band rather than extrapolated into its ends.
